summary: This brief post contains two short items. First, Joel notes that when Atomz.com started charging $100 for three months of search service, he switched to Google in 20 minutes, illustrating how easily replaceable commodity services are when free alternatives exist. Second, he makes a tongue-in-cheek joke connecting a new hire at Fog Creek Software with Nortel's stock plunge, humorously implying causation.
key_points: Commodity web services with free alternatives have zero pricing power — users will switch instantly rather than pay
Google's free search offering made paid search providers like Atomz immediately obsolete
Joel uses humor to celebrate new hires and build Fog Creek's brand as a desirable employer
money_quotes: When Atomz asked me to start paying ($100 for three months), it took me 20 minutes to switch over to Google. Kiss them goodbye.
key_insight: When a free, superior alternative exists, charging for a commodity service is a death sentence — users will leave in minutes.
